segments.title: Kynda class as Gorshkov's disruptive missile-cruiser template
segments.summary: The Kynda class is framed as Gorshkov's first large post-Stalin missile-cruiser statement: small in numbers, but disruptive in reach, politics, and fleet design.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke places the Project 58 cruisers in the political transition from Kuznetsov to Gorshkov and the post-Stalin Soviet Navy. The ships' Shaddock anti-ship missiles made even a small hull a long-range problem for carrier groups, convoys, ports, and naval diplomacy. The lecture treats the class as both a technological test bed and a strategic signal, linking missile armament, helicopter thinking, conservative ASW weapons, and Gorshkov's broader blue-water ambitions. The closing argument is that surface raiders did not need to win a war by themselves to matter; they could divide Allied attention and complicate reinforcement across the Atlantic.

evidence.note: Introduces the Project 58/Kynda class as the first large Soviet ships after Stalin, tied to Gorshkov's rise and different view of big ships.
Explains how the Shaddock missile fit gave the class long-range anti-ship, convoy, port, and possible nuclear-threat implications.
Describes the class as specialist troublemakers, technological test beds, and early evidence of Soviet helicopter and conservative ASW thinking.
Connects the Kynda class to Soviet blue-water presence, surface-raider disruption, and Allied Atlantic reinforcement vulnerabilities.
